seo.title: Building Databases for AI Apps with open-source tools
seo.description: A step-by-step implementation guide for Databases for AI Apps with open-source tools.

content.intro: This guide details the implementation of a production-grade database architecture for AI applications using PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension and Drizzle ORM. It focuses on the transition from prototype-level vector storage to a scalable, type-safe infrastructure capable of handling hybrid relational and semantic queries in serverless environments.
prerequisites: A PostgreSQL instance with pgvector support (e.g., Neon, Supabase, or self-hosted v15+)
Basic familiarity with SQL and ORM concepts
An API key for an embedding provider (e.g., OpenAI, Voyage AI) to generate vector data

steps.title: Enable Vector Extensions and Schema Initialization
steps.explanation: Before storing embeddings, the pgvector extension must be explicitly enabled in your database. This step also involves setting up the initial database schema to house both structured metadata and high-dimensional vectors.
pitfalls: Specifying the wrong vector dimension (e.g., using 1536 for a model that outputs 768) will cause insertion failures.
Forgetting to enable the extension in the 'public' schema or the specific schema used by your application.

steps.title: Define Type-Safe Schema with Drizzle ORM
steps.explanation: Map the database structure to your application code using Drizzle ORM. This ensures that vector operations are type-checked and that your metadata fields are correctly handled as JSONB.
pitfalls: Drizzle does not have native vector type support in all versions; custom types are required to handle the cast between JS arrays and Postgres vector strings.

steps.title: Configure Connection Pooling for Serverless
steps.explanation: AI applications often run on serverless functions (Vercel, AWS Lambda) which can quickly exhaust Postgres connection limits. Use a connection pooler like PgBouncer or a driver-level pooler like @neondatabase/serverless.
pitfalls: Using default 'prepare: true' settings with external poolers like PgBouncer (transaction mode) causes 'prepared statement already exists' errors.

steps.title: Implement HNSW Indexing for Performance
steps.explanation: Standard IVFFlat indexes require a minimum amount of data before they become effective. HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) indexes are generally preferred for AI apps due to higher recall and better performance without needing a training step.
pitfalls: Creating HNSW indexes on large datasets is memory-intensive; ensure your database instance has sufficient RAM relative to the index size.
Choosing 'vector_l2_ops' instead of 'vector_cosine_ops' if your embedding model is optimized for cosine similarity.

steps.title: Execute Hybrid Search Queries
steps.explanation: Combine semantic search with relational filters. This pattern allows you to find relevant content while restricting results by user ID, date ranges, or categories stored in the metadata.
pitfalls: Stringifying large embedding arrays inside SQL templates can lead to query size limits; use parameterized inputs or binary formats where supported.
Distance operators (<=> for cosine, <-> for L2) return distance, not similarity; subtract from 1 for cosine similarity.

content.conclusion: You have implemented a robust database foundation for an AI application. By using PostgreSQL with pgvector, you avoid the complexity of managing a separate vector database while maintaining high-performance semantic search. For next steps, monitor index bloat and consider partitioning your tables if your document count exceeds 10 million records.
